# Code review iteration

Full review of github.com/kamilsk/check at commit `63f970e 2021-01-06` (HEAD of main): README, go.mod/go.sum, all 18 .go files (main, cmd, errors, http/availability, tools), Makefile, .golangci.yml, .travis.yml, .goreleaser.yml, and the vendored colly v2.1.0 sources where the report builder's assumptions depend on library behavior. Review angle: real defects first, then idiomaticity against 2026 Go practice, then toolchain currency, as input for a modernization pass. `.github/reviews/` did not exist before this iteration, so there are no previous review files to match against; `.github/issues/` and `.github/roadmap/` were ignored as offline backlog dumps.

Severity: **Crit** / **High** / **Med** / **Low** / **Nit**.
Class: `correctness` · `security` · `concurrency` · `perf` · `api` · `idiom` · `modern` · `test` · `build` · `docs`.

## 1. Panics exit with code 1 and no diagnostics at all

Anchor: main.go:43-51, errors/wrapper.go:16-24.

```go
defer func() {
    errors.Recover(&err)
    if err != nil {
        // so, when `issue` project will be ready
        // I have to integrate it to open GitHub issues
        // with stack trace from terminal
        app.Shutdown(failed)
    }
}()
```

`errors.Recover` captures any panic into `err`, and then the error is discarded: nothing is ever written to `app.Output` before `Shutdown(failed)` fires. The codebase deliberately panics on internal inconsistencies (`report.go:145` for unknown events, plus the nil dereference pinned as expected behavior in `report_test.go:144`), so the user-visible result of any such bug is exit code 1 with completely empty stderr. The comment admits the reporting half was postponed until a future `issue` project — five years later it still swallows everything. Debugging a field report of "check just exits silently" is close to impossible.

**Fix.** Print before exiting: `fmt.Fprintf(app.Output, "check: %+v\n", err)` in the deferred block (with `%+v` the pkg/errors stack, while it still exists, would even be included). Better: stop recovering panics entirely — a panic in a CLI should crash loudly with its stack trace; recover-and-mask is strictly worse than the default runtime behavior.

## 2. Toolchain, CI, and release pipeline are frozen in 2020

Anchor: go.mod:3, .travis.yml:13-17, .golangci.yml:4-13, .goreleaser.yml:10-26, tools/tools.go:3, README.md:64-68.

Everything infrastructural is from the Go 1.15 era and much of it is not merely old but dead:

- **CI is nonexistent.** `.travis.yml` targets travis-ci.org, which shut down in mid-2021. The README badge points at it. Nothing builds, tests, or lints this repo today.
- **go.mod says `go 1.15`** (current: Go 1.26 era). Sources still use `io/ioutil` (deprecated since 1.16; `printer.go:7`), `interface{}` instead of `any`, `// +build tools` instead of `//go:build`, `sort.Sort` with hand-written `Len/Less/Swap` (`printer.go:209-223`) instead of `slices.SortFunc`.
- **Dependencies:** `github.com/pkg/errors` is archived (see [[#7. Custom errors package proxies archived pkg errors and its stacks go nowhere]]); cobra 1.1.1 predates the built-in `completion` command (1.2+), so all of `cmd/completion.go` is now redundant; colly 2.1.0, spinner 1.11.1, color 1.9.0, testify 1.6.1 are all 5+ major-CVE-cycles behind. The `vendor/` directory plus `GOFLAGS=-mod=vendor` is a pre-proxy habit that mostly adds diff noise for a leaf CLI.
- **Lint config is unloadable:** `.golangci.yml` enables `deadcode`, `structcheck`, `varcheck` (removed from golangci-lint in v1.49) and predates the v2 config format; the commented list references a dozen more deprecated linters (`golint`, `interfacer`, `scopelint`, `maligned`, `exhaustivestruct`).
- **Release/install chain:** `.goreleaser.yml` uses `replacements`, `brews.folder`, and `brews.github`, all removed in modern GoReleaser (`--rm-dist`/`--skip-publish` flags in `Makefile:168` likewise); `bin/install` was generated by godownloader, archived since 2021, and README's `go get -u github.com/kamilsk/check` stopped installing binaries in Go 1.17.
- **Missing safety nets:** no GitHub Actions, no dependabot, no `govulncheck`, no CodeQL.

**Fix.** One modernization PR, in this order: (1) bump `go.mod` to a current Go version, drop `vendor/`, run `go get -u ./... && go mod tidy`; (2) replace Travis with a GitHub Actions workflow (build + `go test -race` + golangci-lint + govulncheck) and add dependabot for gomod/actions; (3) rewrite `.golangci.yml` for the v2 config; (4) regenerate `.goreleaser.yml` against current GoReleaser and delete `bin/install`; (5) delete `cmd/completion.go` in favor of cobra's built-in command; (6) migrate `tools/` to the Go 1.24+ `tool` directive in go.mod; (7) mechanical sweep: `ioutil`→`os`/`io`, `interface{}`→`any`, `//go:build`, `slices.SortFunc`; (8) fix README install instructions (`go install ...@latest`).

## 3. Exit code stays 0 when broken links or site errors are found

Anchor: http/availability/printer.go:117-161 (the `return nil` at printer.go:160), cmd/urls.go:40-49.

`Printer.Print` walks the report, prints 404/500 links in red, prints `report %q has error %q` for sites that could not be crawled at all — and then unconditionally returns `nil`. `urlsCmd.RunE` returns that value, so `check urls :bad` and `check urls https://site-full-of-dead-links` both exit 0. For a link checker, the exit code is the primary machine-readable output; as-is the tool cannot gate a CI pipeline or a cron job, which is the most natural use of "fast website link checker". A related paper cut: site-level errors are printed to the same writer as the report (`w`, stdout), not stderr, so `check urls ... | grep '\[4..\]'` silently swallows them.

**Fix.** Track failures while printing (any `site.Error`, any link with `StatusCode == 0 || StatusCode >= 400`, optionally problems) and return a sentinel error, or have `Print` return a summary struct that `RunE` converts into a non-nil error / `os.Exit(2)`. Route diagnostics through `cmd.ErrOrStderr()`. Consider a `--fail-on` flag (`error|4xx|3xx`) for tunable strictness.

## 4. Report builder crashes the process on inconsistent event streams

Anchor: http/availability/report.go:145, 151, 157-162.

```go
link := links[linkLocation]
page := pages[pageLocation]
if _, exists := barrier[page][link]; !exists {
    barrier[page][link] = struct{}{}
    {
        link := *link
```

`listen` assumes every `WalkEvent.Href` was preceded by a matching `ResponseEvent`/`ErrorEvent` and every `WalkEvent.Page` by a response for that page. A miss makes `links[linkLocation]` nil and `link := *link` panics; same for `page.Link.Location` at report.go:164 via `hasSameHost`. The project knows: `report_test.go:131-145` pins `"runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference"` as the *expected* outcome of a "not consistent fetch result". `Crawler` and `EventBus` are exported API explicitly designed for third-party implementations ("All implementations must communicate with report builder through the passed EventBus", crawler.go:26-29), yet the contract violation is an unrecoverable process crash — and combined with [[#1. Panics exit with code 1 and no diagnostics at all]] it is a *silent* crash. The unknown-event branch at report.go:145 panics too, and the embedding trick described in [[#10. Sealed event interface built from an embedded nil interface field]] makes it easy to hit from outside the package.

**Fix.** Treat inconsistencies as data, not invariants: when `links[linkLocation]` is nil, synthesize a `Link{Location: linkLocation}` or record a `ProblemEvent` and continue; route unknown events into `Site.Problems` instead of panicking. Panic-as-validation is only defensible for truly unreachable states; states reachable through an exported interface are not that.

## 5. Decode flag erases output lines containing malformed percent-escapes

Anchor: http/availability/printer.go:62-64, 138, 147-150.

```go
p.decoder = func(origin string) string {
    decoded, _ := url.PathUnescape(origin)
    return decoded
}
```

`url.PathUnescape` returns `("", error)` on the first invalid escape sequence. The error is discarded, so with `--decode` any rendered line whose URL contains a literal, malformed `%`-sequence (`%zz`, a lone trailing `%` — common in scraped href attributes) is replaced by an empty string: the link disappears from the report entirely, status code included. Exactly the URLs most likely to be broken are the ones hidden. A second design wrinkle: the decoder runs over the whole rendered line (`p.decoder(buf.String())` at printer.go:138), i.e. over the `[200]`/arrow markup too, not just the URL, so escapes in error texts are also mangled.

**Fix.** Fall back to the original on error: `if decoded, err := url.PathUnescape(origin); err == nil { return decoded }; return origin`. Better, decode per-field (Location, Redirect) inside the template via a `FuncMap` function, so the markup is never run through the decoder.

## 6. Test fixture wires every site to the first chain and silences the linter

Anchor: http/availability/pkg_test.go:155-159.

```go
chain1.handlers = append(chain1.handlers, echoCode, echoLinks(site2.URL, main.URL))
//nolint:gocritic
chain2.handlers = append(chain1.handlers, echoCode, echoLinks(site1.URL, main.URL))
//nolint:gocritic
chain3.handlers = append(chain1.handlers, echoCode, echoLinks(site1.URL, site2.URL))
```

Lines 157 and 159 append to `chain1.handlers` instead of building `chain2`/`chain3` from scratch — a classic `appendAssign` bug. gocritic flagged it and the warning was suppressed with `//nolint:gocritic` instead of being fixed. Result: every server's handler list starts with chain1's `echoLinks(site2, main)`, which matches first for `/`, so the intended three-site cross-link topology (site2 linking to site1, main linking to site1 and site2) is never constructed — site1 is never linked from anywhere. The magic-number assertions in `crawler_test.go:47-51` (28 error events, 37 walk events, ...) were evidently tuned to the accidental topology, so the test is green while verifying the wrong graph, and any fix to the fixture will break the counts in confusing ways.

**Fix.** `chain2.handlers = append(chain2.handlers, ...)`, `chain3.handlers = append(chain3.handlers, ...)`, drop both `nolint` comments, and re-derive the expected event counts from the now-correct topology (ideally asserting on sets of visited URLs rather than bare counters). Policy takeaway: a `nolint` on an *appendAssign* warning is nearly always a real bug — audit before silencing.

## 7. Custom errors package proxies archived pkg errors and its stacks go nowhere

Anchor: errors/wrapper.go:33-42, http/availability/printer.go:126-128.

The in-house `errors` package is a thin proxy over `github.com/pkg/errors`, which has been archived since 2021; the standard library has covered wrapping (`%w`, `errors.Is/As/Join`) since Go 1.13. The stack-trace machinery it exists for is doubly inert: `StackTrace` uses a direct type assertion (`errors/wrapper.go:38`) without unwrapping, and the dominant wrap path `errors.WithMessage` (used by the crawler, crawler.go:51) produces a `*withMessage` that has no `StackTrace` method — so for real crawl errors it always returns nil. And on the one path where a stack does survive (`Wrapf` in `NewSite`), the printer writes it to `ioutil.Discard` "for future" (printer.go:127) — dead code shipping to users.

**Fix.** Delete the `errors` package. Use `fmt.Errorf("parse entry point URL %q: %w", entry, err)`, `errors.New`, `errors.As`; drop the `Recover` helper together with the recover-and-mask flow from finding 1; delete the Discard write in the printer. This removes a dependency, an indirection layer, and two illusions of functionality in one move.

## 8. Sites channel and half-deep copies complicate a simple report

Anchor: http/availability/report.go:43-68.

`Fill` crawls synchronously, then copies each `Site` into a fully-buffered channel that is closed before `Fill` even returns; `Sites()` hands that channel to the printer. The channel provides no streaming (everything is already done) and no concurrency safety — it is a slice with extra steps, and it makes the report single-consumption: a second `Sites()` call yields an already-drained channel. The defensive copy is also illusory: `copied.Pages[i].Link` still aliases the original `*Link` (report.go:49-56 copies the `Page` values but not the embedded pointer), and each `page.Links[j].Page` back-pointer targets the *original* pages, so a consumer mutating through those pointers reaches shared state anyway — the copying costs allocations without buying isolation.

**Fix.** Have `Fill` return `[]Site` (or `Report.Sites() []Site` over a stored slice) and delete `NewReadableEventBus`'s public cousin machinery. If streaming results page-by-page during the crawl is a future goal, that needs a genuinely concurrent design (crawl goroutine + unbuffered channel + context), not a buffered snapshot. Either way, drop the half-copy: pass values you own, not aliased graphs.

## 9. Fully sequential crawl with no concurrency, rate limit, or overall deadline

Anchor: http/availability/report.go:45-46, http/availability/crawler.go:69.

Sites are crawled strictly one after another (`for _, site := range r.sites { site.Error = site.Fetch(...) }`), and the colly collector runs in its default synchronous mode, so every link on every page is fetched serially. With colly 2.1.0's default 10-second per-request client timeout, a site with a few hundred links, some of them slow or dead, takes tens of minutes; there is no `--timeout`, no context, and no way to bound the whole run except Ctrl+C (which, per finding 1's design, also produces no partial output). For a tool whose README calls it a "fast website link checker", single-flight fetching is the bottleneck, and conversely the absence of `colly.LimitRule` means that if async were switched on naively it would hammer the target host.

**Fix.** Enable `colly.Async(true)` with `c.Limit(&colly.LimitRule{DomainGlob: "*", Parallelism: 4})` and `c.Wait()` before closing the bus; crawl distinct sites in an `errgroup`; plumb `context.Context` from `cobra.Command.Context()` through `Crawler.Visit` (colly supports `Request.Do` cancellation via its context, or set a custom `http.Client` with timeout) and add `--timeout`/`--parallelism` flags. Signal handling (`signal.NotifyContext`) makes Ctrl+C print the partial report instead of dying mute.

## 10. Sealed event interface built from an embedded nil interface field

Anchor: http/availability/report.go:200-244.

```go
type event interface{ family() }

type ErrorEvent struct {
    event
    ...
}
```

Each event type "implements" the sealed `event` interface by embedding the interface itself as a nil field. This compiles, but calling `family()` on any event is an instant nil-pointer panic; every value carries a useless 16-byte field that also breaks `==` comparability guarantees users might expect from plain data structs; and the seal is trivially bypassed by embedding an existing event (exactly what `report_test.go:120-123` does), which then lands in the `default:` panic branch of `listen`. It is a known Go trick, but in 2026 it reads as a trap: the marker interface promises a method that must never be called.

**Fix.** Give each event type a real empty marker method: `func (ErrorEvent) family() {}` etc. Four one-line methods, and every event value is safe, comparable, and genuinely sealed (external types can still embed, so keep the `default:` branch — but route it to `Problems` per finding 4 instead of panicking).

## 11. make hooks copies from a githooks directory that is not in the repo

Anchor: Makefile:196, Makefile:4, Makefile:232.

`GIT_HOOKS = post-merge pre-commit pre-push` is set, so the `hooks:` target (`cp githooks/$$hook .git/hooks/`) and the per-hook templated targets are generated — but there is no `githooks/` directory anywhere in the repository. `make hooks` fails, and since `init: deps test lint hooks`, the documented bootstrap path for a fresh clone (`make init`) fails too. The hook files were evidently never committed (or lived only in the shared octomation/makefiles template repo).

**Fix.** Either commit the three hook scripts under `githooks/`, or unset `GIT_HOOKS` so the conditional block disappears. If the Makefile gets refreshed from current octomation/makefiles during the modernization pass (finding 2), verify the hooks story there instead.

## 12. cmd wiring depends on string lookup of the version command

Anchor: cmd/root.go:22-37, cmd/root.go:17-20, main.go:52-61.

`client()` builds the crawler's User-Agent by walking the parent command's children looking for one whose `Use` is literally `"version"` and reading its `Version` field. That command is registered in a different package (`main.application.Run`), at runtime, before `Execute` — a stringly-typed, order-sensitive coupling: anyone driving `cmd.RootCmd` directly (as the package's own tests do) silently gets a UA without a version. Nearby, `asBool` swallows `strconv.ParseBool` errors, re-implementing what `cmd.Flags().GetBool("verbose")` already does with error reporting. Both are three-line fixes that remove reflection-flavored indirection.

**Fix.** Set `RootCmd.Version` (cobra generates `--version` and templates it) or inject the version string into the `cmd` package via a setter/variable at build wire-up; derive the UA as `"check/" + version`. Replace `asBool(cmd.Flag(...).Value)` with `cmd.Flags().GetBool(...)` and propagate the error from `RunE`.

## 13. README ships placeholder sections and an opaque shortened installer URL

Anchor: README.md:10-16, README.md:56-58.

The "Idea" and "Motivation" sections contain a literal `...` placeholder each, with the actual description delegated to a private-ish Notion link — five years on, a visitor still cannot tell from GitHub what the tool is for beyond one example. The binary install instruction is `curl -sSL https://bit.ly/install-check | sh`: piping a *link-shortener* URL to `sh` is a supply-chain anti-pattern (the target is invisible and silently re-pointable) on top of the script itself being generated by the archived godownloader (finding 2). The Travis badge and `go get -u` instructions are covered there as well.

**Fix.** Write the two paragraphs inline (the `check urls` example already tells most of the story); replace the bit.ly pipe with a direct GitHub Releases link or `go install github.com/kamilsk/check@latest`; refresh badges when CI comes back.
